name: DeLorean
description:
Founded by American automotive executive John DeLorean in 1975, DeLorean Motor Company (DMC) was a short-lived but iconic manufacturer that produced only one model: the DeLorean DMC-12. The company faced numerous financial challenges and production issues, but the DMC-12 gained notoriety in popular culture due to its appearance in the Back to the Future movie trilogy. Despite the company's bankruptcy in 1982, the DeLorean brand has maintained a cult following. Currently, there are no actively produced models by DeLorean. 

The founding of the company was preceded by John DeLorean's successful tenure at General Motors where he led the development of the Pontiac GTO and Firebird. After leaving GM in 1968, DeLorean co-founded the Automobile Technology Company (ATC) with British businessman Harold Wilson. ATC was initially established to develop a mid-engine sports car but faced financial difficulties. 

In 1975, John DeLorean established DMC in Dunmurry, Northern Ireland, with an aim to produce the DeLorean DMC-12, designed by Italian automotive designer Giorgetto Giugiaro. The car featured gull-wing doors and a stainless-steel body, making it a standout in the automotive industry.

Production began in 1981 but was plagued with issues such as quality control problems, delays, and financial troubles. By 1982, DMC filed for bankruptcy, ending production of the DeLorean DMC-12.
